behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: The core failure is that a real LLM might generate a generic response despite having the email thread as context. A mock LLM would always return a well-crafted specific response, hiding this failure. Only a real LLM call reveals whether generation is actually context-aware and not just prompted to be.
behaviors.test_description: Call the generation endpoint with a real LLM backend (not mocked) and a specific email thread containing named people, technical details, and prior discussion points. Verify the generated draft includes at least 3 specific facts from the incoming email (e.g., sender's name, specific problem mentioned, prior resolution referenced). Run multiple times to detect systematic failures or variance in context incorporation.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: Factual consistency checking typically relies on semantic understanding or LLM-based classification. A mock consistency checker would always correctly identify contradictions; the real system might miss subtle ones or misclassify. Mocking the KB hides edge cases. Real KB data and real checking logic (whether rule-based, embedding-based, or LLM-based) are necessary to catch actual failure modes in the checking algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a real knowledge base with specific facts. Create test drafts with claims that clearly contradict KB entries, align with them, and represent partial/ambiguous contradictions. Run the consistency check with real LLM or semantic backend (not mocks). Verify contradictory claims are flagged, aligned claims pass, and edge cases (partial matches, negations, implicit contradictions) are handled correctly.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: This tests LLM's ability to acknowledge knowledge gaps and resist hallucination—a fundamental failure mode. A mock LLM can always return a disclaimer; a real LLM might generate plausible-sounding fabricated facts when faced with a knowledge gap. Mocking would hide hallucination. Only a real LLM facing an empty/irrelevant KB exposes this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the KB with no entries relevant to the email thread's domain. Call draft generation with a real LLM (not mock). Verify the draft includes an explicit disclaimer (e.g., 'I don't have information about X', 'I cannot confirm these details') rather than fabricated facts. Check that disclaimers precede or replace would-be fabricated claims. Run multiple times to measure hallucination frequency under real conditions.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: The failure is a coordination bug: code sends the draft before or without checking approval. A mock approval function silently passes, hiding the bug. Testing requires a real (or realistic) blocking approval mechanism—queue, event, or awaited promise that actually blocks. This is local integration (multiple components wired together), not requiring external I/O or LLM calls. The test verifies the blocking guarantee and absence of auto-send.
behaviors.test_description: Set up an approval queue or event system (in-memory, not external APIs). Trigger the full pipeline to draft generation and approval stage. Before approval, verify the draft is held in the queue and not sent to any outbox/recipient list. Simulate user approval; verify draft is marked approved and subsequently sent. Test concurrent scenarios: multiple pending drafts, rapid approve/reject, approval timeouts.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: This is a multi-step pipeline with at least 2 LLM-dependent steps (draft generation and fact-checking). Mocking any step hides integration failures, timing issues, or cascading errors. Real LLM calls at multiple stages are necessary. The interaction between agentic steps (LLM generation → LLM-based fact-checking → approval coordination) defines a workflow boundary—failures only surface when all steps run in sequence with real dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full pipeline end-to-end with a realistic multi-message email thread (mixed senders, specific technical topics, prior discussion). Use real LLM backends for both generation and fact-checking. Validate at each stage: (1) thread is parsed and understood, (2) draft is generated and contextual, (3) fact-check runs and reports issues accurately, (4) draft is presented without auto-send, (5) draft is sent only after approval. Verify outputs match expected formats and dependencies flow correctly.
